title: Richard Cornish executed for sodomy in the Virginia Colony
description: Richard Cornish of the Virginia Colony was tried and executed for sodomy, one of the earliest recorded capital sodomy cases in colonial America.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Massachusetts Bay Colony makes sodomy a punishable crime
description: The Massachusetts Bay General Court declared sodomy, along with fornication, adultery and rape, to be crimes subject to punishment.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: First recorded conviction of women for same-sex conduct in the colonies
description: Sarah White Norman and Mary Vincent Hammon were charged with lewd behavior together in Plymouth, Massachusetts, in what is regarded as the first conviction of two women for same-sex conduct in colonial America.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Sodomy laws entrenched across the American colonies
description: By this period sodomy statutes were established throughout the early American colonies and the colonial militia, criminalizing same-sex acts.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: First documented U.S. military discharge for homosexuality
description: Lieutenant Gotthold Frederick Enslin was dismissed from the Continental Army for homosexuality, the first documented case of a military discharge for that reason in American history.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Jefferson proposes replacing death penalty for sodomy in Virginia
description: Thomas Jefferson proposed a Virginia law that would punish sodomy by mutilation instead of death; the Virginia legislature rejected the measure.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Equiano narrative references same-sex relationships in slave culture
description: Olaudah Equiano, a formerly enslaved man, published an autobiographical narrative that has been read as describing same-sex relationships within enslaved communities.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Early printed use of the word lesbian by William King
description: The English writer William King used the term lesbian in his satirical work The Toast, an early appearance of the word in print.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Chamberlain and Chaffee begin a lifelong partnership in California
description: Jason Chamberlain and John Chaffee traveled from Boston to California and lived together as lifelong partners in Groveland until Chamberlain's death in 1903.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Men-only miners ball reported in Gold Rush California
description: The traveler J.D. Borthwick described attending a men-only dance, a so-called miners ball, in Angels Camp, California, a glimpse of same-sex social life in Gold Rush mining camps.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: James Buchanan serves as president amid a long same-sex partnership
description: James Buchanan, the only lifelong bachelor to serve as U.S. president, had a long and close relationship with William Rufus King from 1840 to 1853, often cited in histories of same-sex intimacy in American public life.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Sarah Emma Edmonds serves in the Union Army as Franklin Thompson
description: Sarah Emma Edmonds adopted the identity of Franklin Thompson to enlist in the Union Army, among an estimated 400 documented people assigned female who lived as men to serve in the Civil War.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Jennie Hodgers serves three years as Albert Cashier
description: Jennie Hodgers enlisted in the Union Army in Illinois under the name Albert Cashier and fought for three years, continuing to live as a man afterward.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: We'wha, a Zuni Two-Spirit, meets the U.S. president
description: We'wha, a Zuni Two-Spirit person, traveled to Washington and met President Grover Cleveland, becoming one of the most documented Two-Spirit figures of the era. The source dates this to 1868, but it is generally placed in 1886.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Karl-Maria Kertbeny coins the term homosexual
description: The Hungarian journalist Karl-Maria Kertbeny introduced the term homosexual, publishing it in a German-language pamphlet that argued against the criminalization of same-sex acts.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Death of Charley Parkhurst reveals a life lived as a man
description: Charley Parkhurst, a well-known California stagecoach driver, was found after death to have been assigned female at birth while living as a man throughout adult life.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Henry James novel popularizes the phrase Boston marriage
description: Henry James published The Bostonians, depicting a long-term relationship between two women; the term Boston marriage came into use for such committed female partnerships.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: The term lesbian enters a medical dictionary
description: The word lesbian appeared in a medical dictionary for the first time, reflecting the growing clinical framing of same-sex desire among women.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Jane Addams founds Hull House alongside partner Mary Rozet Smith
description: Jane Addams established the Hull House settlement in Chicago, supported by her longtime companion and partner Mary Rozet Smith, whose relationship is a notable example of a same-sex partnership in the era.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Birth of Alan Hart, transgender physician and radiology pioneer
description: Alan Hart, later a physician who pioneered the use of X-ray screening for tuberculosis and one of the first documented American transgender men, was born.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Early printed use of the term bisexual in Psychopathia Sexualis
description: An English translation of the German sexology text Psychopathia Sexualis carried one of the earliest printed uses of the term bisexual.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Oscar Wilde convicted of gross indecency in London
description: Oscar Wilde was tried in London for gross indecency, convicted, and sentenced to two years of imprisonment, a widely publicized case that shaped attitudes toward homosexuality.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Magnus Hirschfeld publishes Sappho and Socrates
description: Magnus Hirschfeld issued the pamphlet Sappho and Socrates, an early defense of same-sex love that anticipated his later work advocating homosexual rights.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Gertrude Stein meets Alice B. Toklas in Paris
description: Gertrude Stein met Alice B. Toklas in Paris, beginning a lifelong partnership and a salon that drew together many writers and artists of the period.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Hirschfeld testifies that homosexuality is part of nature
description: At a German trial, Magnus Hirschfeld testified that homosexuality was a natural part of human variation, using the courtroom to advance his scientific and legal arguments.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Harlem Renaissance becomes a hub of queer cultural life
description: The Harlem Renaissance flourished in New York, a movement historians have described as being as queer as it was Black, with many prominent artists and writers living openly non-heterosexual lives.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Hirschfeld founds the Institute for Sexual Science in Berlin
description: Magnus Hirschfeld established the Institute for Sexual Science in Berlin, a pioneering center for research, advocacy and care that became central to the early homosexual rights movement.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Henry Gerber founds the first U.S. gay rights organization
description: Henry Gerber established the Society for Human Rights in Chicago, recognized as the first gay rights organization in the United States, though it was quickly suppressed.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Radclyffe Hall publishes The Well of Loneliness
description: Radclyffe Hall published the novel The Well of Loneliness, a landmark work of lesbian literature that provoked obscenity disputes and public debate in the United States and England.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Nazis raid and destroy the Institute for Sexual Science
description: Nazi storm troopers raided the Institute for Sexual Science in Berlin and seized its library of more than 12,000 books and 35,000 images, which were publicly burned days later.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Nazi Germany imprisons homosexual men in concentration camps
description: Under Nazi rule an estimated 100,000 homosexual men in Germany were arrested and many were sent to concentration camps, where they were forced to wear the pink triangle.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Gay and lesbian Americans enlist after Pearl Harbor
description: Following the attack on Pearl Harbor, large numbers of gay and lesbian Americans joined the military, part of a wartime surge in enlistment reported to reach 14,000 people per day in 1942.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Allied forces liberate Nazi concentration camps
description: United States, British and Soviet forces liberated the people held in Nazi concentration camps, including prisoners persecuted for homosexuality.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Homosexual prisoners are among the last freed after liberation
description: Men imprisoned for homosexuality were among the last groups released after the liberation of the camps, because Paragraph 175 of the German Criminal Code kept their conduct criminal.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Kinsey report documents male homosexual behavior
description: Alfred Kinsey published Sexual Behavior in the Human Male, presenting data on the prevalence of male same-sex behavior that challenged prevailing assumptions.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: U.S. Congress labels homosexuals as security risks
description: A congressional report titled Employment of Homosexuals and Other Sex Perverts in Government characterized homosexuals as security risks, helping to launch the purge later known as the Lavender Scare.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Harry Hay founds the Mattachine Society
description: Harry Hay and others formed the Mattachine Society in Los Angeles, one of the first sustained gay rights organizations in the United States.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Christine Jorgensen becomes a widely known transgender woman
description: Christine Jorgensen underwent gender-affirming surgery and became a prominent public figure, bringing unprecedented visibility to transgender lives in the United States.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: APA classifies homosexuality as a mental disorder
description: The American Psychiatric Association listed homosexuality as a sociopathic personality disturbance in its first diagnostic manual, formalizing a pathologizing view that stood for two decades.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: U.S. immigration law bars homosexual entrants
description: Congress passed an Immigration Act barring people with a psychopathic personality, a phrase officials clarified was meant to exclude homosexuals from entering the country.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Kinsey report documents female homosexual behavior
description: Alfred Kinsey published Sexual Behavior in the Human Female, extending his earlier research to include data on same-sex behavior among women.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Executive Order 10450 bars homosexuals from federal jobs
description: President Dwight D. Eisenhower issued Executive Order 10450, barring homosexuals from federal government employment as security risks and intensifying the Lavender Scare.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Daughters of Bilitis founded as first U.S. lesbian organization
description: Phyllis Lyon and Del Martin founded the Daughters of Bilitis in San Francisco, the first lesbian rights organization in the United States.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Frank Kameny dismissed from federal service for homosexuality
description: The astronomer Frank Kameny was fired from the U.S. Army Map Service because he was gay; his unsuccessful appeal to the Supreme Court set him on a path to lifelong activism.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Supreme Court protects a gay magazine in One v. Olesen
description: In One v. Olesen the Supreme Court protected the free-speech rights of ONE Magazine, ruling that its gay-themed content was not obscene and could be sent through the mail.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Frank Kameny establishes a Washington Mattachine branch
description: Frank Kameny formed a Washington, D.C. branch of the Mattachine Society, building an organizational base for a more confrontational style of gay rights activism.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Illinois becomes first U.S. state to decriminalize sodomy
description: Illinois became the first state to decriminalize consensual same-sex acts between adults in private, removing sodomy from its criminal code.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Bayard Rustin organizes the March on Washington
description: Bayard Rustin, an openly gay Black civil rights strategist and close associate of Martin Luther King Jr., was the chief organizer of the 1963 March on Washington.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Compton's Cafeteria Riot in San Francisco
description: Transgender people and drag queens fought back against police harassment at Compton's Cafeteria in San Francisco, an uprising that helped lead to the National Transsexual Counseling Unit.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Oscar Wilde Memorial Bookshop opens in New York
description: Craig Rodwell opened the Oscar Wilde Memorial Bookshop in New York City, the first U.S. bookstore devoted to gay literature and rights.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Stonewall uprising in New York City
description: Patrons of the Stonewall Inn in New York City resisted a police raid, sparking several nights of protest that became a turning point for the modern LGBTQ rights movement.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Gay Liberation Front forms after Stonewall
description: The Gay Liberation Front was organized in New York in the wake of the Stonewall uprising, adopting a militant approach to gay liberation.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Gay Activists Alliance founded in New York
description: The Gay Activists Alliance formed in New York to secure basic rights and freedoms for gay people through focused political action.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: First gay pride marches mark the Stonewall anniversary
description: On the first anniversary of the Stonewall uprising, gay pride marches were held in several U.S. cities, establishing the tradition of annual pride demonstrations.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: The Body Politic begins publishing in Toronto
description: The Body Politic began publication in Toronto, growing into one of the most widely read LGBTQ rights periodicals in North America.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: National Bisexual Liberation Group forms in New York
description: The National Bisexual Liberation Group was founded in New York, an early organization dedicated specifically to bisexual visibility and community.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Jonathan Ned Katz stages the play Coming Out
description: The documentary play Coming Out, written by Jonathan Ned Katz, was performed for the first time in New York, dramatizing gay and lesbian history.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: APA removes homosexuality from its list of disorders
description: The American Psychiatric Association removed homosexuality from its list of mental disorders after sustained advocacy by activists including Frank Kameny and Mattachine Society members.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Elaine Noble is first openly gay person elected to a state legislature
description: Elaine Noble became the first openly gay candidate elected to a state legislature in the United States, serving two terms in the Massachusetts House of Representatives.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Bisexual Forum and Gay American Indians organizations founded
description: The Bisexual Forum was founded in New York City and the Gay American Indians organization was founded in San Francisco, broadening the movement to bisexual and Native American communities.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Jonathan Ned Katz publishes Gay American History
description: Jonathan Ned Katz published Gay American History, one of the first books to document the history of lesbians and gay men in the United States.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Harvey Milk elected in San Francisco
description: Harvey Milk won election as a San Francisco county supervisor, becoming one of the first openly gay elected officials in the United States.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Quebec bans discrimination based on sexual orientation
description: Quebec adopted laws prohibiting discrimination on the basis of sexual orientation in the public and private sectors, an early such protection in North America.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Anita Bryant campaign repeals a Florida gay rights ordinance
description: Anita Bryant led the Save Our Children campaign that successfully repealed a Dade County, Florida ordinance protecting gay people from discrimination, energizing an anti-gay backlash.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: California voters defeat the Briggs Initiative
description: California voters rejected the Briggs Initiative by a margin of 58 percent; the measure would have barred gay and lesbian people from working in public schools.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Rainbow flag first flown at San Francisco pride
description: The rainbow flag, designed as a symbol of gay pride, was flown for the first time during the Gay Freedom Day parade in San Francisco.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Harvey Milk assassinated in San Francisco
description: Harvey Milk and Mayor George Moscone were assassinated at San Francisco City Hall; the assailant, Dan White, was convicted of voluntary manslaughter, a verdict that provoked widespread anger.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: First National March on Washington for gay and lesbian rights
description: More than 100,000 people gathered for the first National March on Washington for Lesbian and Gay Rights, a major show of national political strength.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: PFLAG chapters spread across the United States
description: Chapters of Parents and Friends of Lesbians and Gays were established across the country, building a national network of family support and advocacy.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: First AIDS cases reported in the United States
description: The U.S. Centers for Disease Control reported the first cases of a rare illness later identified as AIDS, marking the start of an epidemic that reshaped LGBTQ communities and activism.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: San Francisco AIDS Foundation is founded
description: The San Francisco AIDS Foundation was co-founded by Cleve Jones, Marcus Conant, Frank Jacobson and Richard Keller to provide services and advocacy during the epidemic.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Perry Watkins challenges his discharge in Watkins v. U.S. Army
description: A federal appeals court ruled in favor of Perry Watkins in his challenge to his discharge from the U.S. Army, a notable case against the military ban on gay service members.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Rock Hudson dies of AIDS-related illness
description: The film star Rock Hudson died of AIDS-related complications and was revealed to be gay, a death that drew major public attention to the epidemic.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Cleve Jones conceives the AIDS Memorial Quilt
description: Cleve Jones conceived and began the AIDS Memorial Quilt, a growing memorial that commemorated people who died of AIDS and became a powerful tool of remembrance and advocacy.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Supreme Court upholds sodomy law in Bowers v. Hardwick
description: In Bowers v. Hardwick the Supreme Court upheld Georgia's sodomy law as constitutional, a major setback that stood until it was overturned in 2003.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: ACT UP founded to confront the AIDS crisis
description: The AIDS Coalition to Unleash Power, known as ACT UP, was formed in New York to press for treatment, research and legislation through direct-action protest.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: First World AIDS Day observed
description: The World Health Organization designated December 1 as the first World AIDS Day, establishing an annual global observance to raise awareness of the epidemic.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Clinton signs the Don't Ask, Don't Tell policy
description: President Bill Clinton signed the Don't Ask, Don't Tell policy, a compromise that allowed gay and lesbian Americans to serve in the military only if they concealed their sexual orientation.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Olympic diver Greg Louganis comes out publicly
description: The four-time Olympic gold medal diver Greg Louganis publicly came out as gay, later recounting his life in the 1996 autobiography Breaking the Surface.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Ellen DeGeneres comes out on national television
description: Ellen DeGeneres came out publicly on The Oprah Winfrey Show and her sitcom character came out as well, making it the first U.S. prime-time series led by an openly gay lead character.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Murder of Matthew Shepard
description: Matthew Shepard, a 21-year-old University of Wyoming student, was attacked and killed in an anti-gay hate crime; the case fueled a campaign that led to the federal Hate Crimes Prevention Act in 2009.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Supreme Court strikes down sodomy laws in Lawrence v. Texas
description: In Lawrence v. Texas the Supreme Court ruled that laws criminalizing consensual same-sex conduct were unconstitutional, overturning Bowers v. Hardwick nationwide.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: California voters pass Proposition 8
description: California voters approved Proposition 8 with 52 percent support, amending the state constitution to define marriage as between a man and a woman and halting same-sex marriages in the state.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Obama signs the repeal of Don't Ask, Don't Tell
description: President Barack Obama signed legislation repealing Don't Ask, Don't Tell, allowing gay and lesbian Americans to serve openly in the military; veteran activist Frank Kameny attended the signing.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Supreme Court clears the way for marriages in Hollingsworth v. Perry
description: In Hollingsworth v. Perry the Supreme Court let stand a lower-court ruling against Proposition 8, effectively restoring same-sex marriage in California.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Supreme Court strikes down part of DOMA in U.S. v. Windsor
description: In United States v. Windsor the Supreme Court ruled the Defense of Marriage Act's federal definition of marriage unconstitutional, extending federal recognition to same-sex marriages.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History

title: Supreme Court legalizes same-sex marriage in Obergefell v. Hodges
description: In Obergefell v. Hodges the Supreme Court ruled that same-sex couples have a constitutional right to marry, requiring all states to license and recognize their marriages.

sources.label: LGBTQ History — LGBT Rights Timeline in American History
